name: Copper
name_origin: From Latin 'cuprum', a contraction of 'aes Cyprium' meaning 'metal of Cyprus', the island that was a major source of copper in the Roman era.

occurrence.abundance_summary: Copper is the 25th most abundant element in Earth's crust at roughly 50-70 ppm. It occurs as native metal and in numerous sulfide and oxide ores; major producing countries include Chile, Peru, China, and the United States.

compounds.name: Copper(II) sulfate
compounds.summary: Bright blue crystalline salt used as a fungicide, algicide, and in electroplating baths.

compounds.name: Copper(I) oxide
compounds.summary: A red semiconducting solid used as a pigment, antifouling agent in marine paints, and in rectifier diodes.

compounds.name: Copper(II) oxide
compounds.summary: Black solid used as a pigment in ceramics and as a precursor for many copper compounds.

compounds.name: Copper(II) chloride
compounds.summary: Yellow-brown salt used as a catalyst in organic synthesis and in the Wacker process.

compounds.name: Copper(II) hydroxide
compounds.summary: Blue gelatinous solid used as a fungicide and as a precursor to copper oxychloride pigments.

uses: Electrical wiring and power transmission due to high conductivity
Plumbing tubing and roofing in construction
Alloying with zinc to make brass and with tin to make bronze
Printed circuit boards and electronic interconnects
reactivity_summary: Copper is a relatively unreactive metal that resists corrosion in dry air but slowly forms a green patina (basic copper carbonate) in moist atmospheres containing CO2 or sulfur compounds. It does not react with non-oxidizing acids but dissolves in oxidizing acids such as nitric acid and hot concentrated sulfuric acid. Copper readily forms colored Cu(I) and Cu(II) complexes.
isotopes_summary: Copper has two stable isotopes: 63Cu (~69.15%) and 65Cu (~30.85%). About 27 radioisotopes have been characterized; the longest-lived is 67Cu (half-life ~61.8 hours), and 64Cu (half-life ~12.7 hours) is used in positron emission tomography and radiotherapy.

citations.source: CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 102nd ed.
